algorithms.name: Binary Tree DFS (Traversals)
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(h) · h = tree height, O(log n) balanced, O(n) skewed
algorithms.analogy: Exploring a family tree — pre-order visits the parent first, in-order visits in family-age order, post-order handles children before the parent.
algorithms.story: You're documenting a company org chart. Pre-order: write down the CEO first, then recurse through their reports (top-down). Post-order: document each team before their manager (bottom-up — used by compilers to evaluate expressions). In-order on a BST: gives employees sorted alphabetically.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Pre-order: serialize/copy a tree, prefix expression evaluation
• In-order: BST traversal (produces sorted output), validate BST
• Post-order: delete a tree, evaluate postfix expression, calculate tree height
• Any problem requiring visiting every node exactly once
algorithms.howItWorks:
Pre-order (Root → Left → Right):
1. Visit root
2. Recurse left subtree
3. Recurse right subtree

In-order (Left → Root → Right):
1. Recurse left subtree
2. Visit root
3. Recurse right subtree

Post-order (Left → Right → Root):
1. Recurse left subtree
2. Recurse right subtree
3. Visit root
algorithms.interviewTips:
• In-order of a BST gives sorted output — use this for 'kth smallest in BST' problems
• Post-order is 'bottom-up' — perfect for height, diameter, path sum problems
• For iterative traversal: pre-order uses a stack; in-order uses a stack with a 'go left' pointer
• Tree height: left and right subtree heights are computed post-order (returned up the call stack)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Expression Tree Evaluation","desc":"Compilers parse arithmetic expressions into trees — in-order gives infix notation, post-order gives postfix (RPN) for stack-based evaluation."},{"title":"XML/HTML DOM Parsing","desc":"Browser rendering engines traverse the DOM tree in pre-order to apply CSS and in post-order to calculate layout sizes."},{"title":"File System Size Calculation","desc":"du (disk usage) command uses post-order traversal — first recurse into all subdirectories, then sum their sizes at the parent level."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting the null base case — every recursive tree function needs if (root == null) return","Confusing traversal order: in-order is Left-Root-Right NOT Root-Left-Right","Height vs depth: height is measured from node to leaf (post-order), depth is from root to node (pre-order)","Modifying a tree during traversal — collect changes in a list, apply after traversal"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Level-Order (BFS)","desc":"Use a Queue to process nodes level by level — produces breadth-first output"},{"name":"Morris Traversal","desc":"O(1) space in-order traversal using thread pointers (no stack/recursion) — advanced interview answer"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    Root["Root (1)"] --> L["Left (2)"]
    Root --> R["Right (3)"]
    L --> LL["(4)"]
    L --> LR["(5)"]
    A["Pre-order: 1,2,4,5,3"] 
    B["In-order:  4,2,5,1,3"]
    C["Post-order:4,5,2,3,1"]
algorithms.keyInsight: Three traversals, one rule: WHERE you visit the root. Pre = Root first (serialize, copy). In = Root middle (BST gives sorted output). Post = Root last (height, diameter — anything needing children's info before parent). Post-order is 'bottom-up' — the return value propagates up the call stack.
algorithms.patternSignal: tree traversal, inorder BST sorted, preorder serialize, postorder height diameter, all nodes once, tree serialization, tree copy, visit every node
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Binary Tree Inorder Traversal","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Nail the recursive and iterative versions — interviewers expect both"},{"name":"Maximum Depth of Binary Tree","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Post-order: 1 + max(left, right)"},{"name":"Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Pre-order traversal — visit, link right, recurse"},{"name":"Diameter of Binary Tree","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Post-order: return height, update global max with left+right"},{"name":"Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Post-order: each node computes max gain from its subtree, updates global max through it"}]

algorithms.name: Binary Search Tree (BST)
algorithms.analogy: A sorted bookshelf where each book knows all books to its left have smaller page counts and all to its right have larger — you never need to scan the whole shelf.
algorithms.story: A BST is a binary tree with one golden rule: for any node X, everything in X's left subtree is smaller than X and everything in X's right subtree is larger. This makes search identical to binary search — but on a dynamic structure that supports insert and delete while maintaining order.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Sorted dynamic set (frequent insertions + sorted order queries)
• Floor/Ceiling queries (nearest smaller/larger element)
• Range queries (all elements between x and y)
• Kth smallest/largest element
• Validate if a binary tree is a valid BST
algorithms.howItWorks:
Search:
1. Start at root. If null → not found
2. If target == node.val → found
3. If target < node.val → recurse left
4. If target > node.val → recurse right

Insert: same as search, insert at the null position
Delete: three cases:
• Node has no children → just remove
• Node has one child → replace node with its child
• Node has two children → find in-order successor (smallest in right subtree), replace value, delete successor
algorithms.interviewTips:
• BST validation: do NOT just check left < root < right locally — pass min/max bounds recursively
• In-order of a valid BST is sorted — use this to find kth smallest or check validity
• Worst case O(n) occurs on sorted input (degenerate tree) — mention AVL/Red-Black as fix
• Delete with 2 children: in-order successor is the leftmost node in the right subtree
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Java TreeMap/TreeSet","desc":"Java's TreeMap is a Red-Black Tree (self-balancing BST) — O(log n) get/put/remove with sorted key iteration."},{"title":"Database B-Tree Index","desc":"MySQL InnoDB uses B-Tree (generalized BST) for all primary key and unique index lookups — BST concepts apply directly."},{"title":"Autocomplete Trie Hybrid","desc":"Some autocomplete systems use BST-backed structures to suggest next words sorted by frequency."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Validating BST locally (just checking child < parent) — misses cases like a node in the left subtree being larger than the grandparent","Forgetting that duplicate handling must be consistent — define whether duplicates go left, right, or are rejected","Not handling the 2-child delete case — the in-order successor approach is the cleanest","Assuming BST operations are always O(log n) — unbalanced trees degrade to O(n)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"AVL Tree","desc":"Self-balancing BST — rotations after insert/delete guarantee O(log n) always"},{"name":"Red-Black Tree","desc":"Loosely balanced BST — used in Java TreeMap; allows slightly unbalanced tree for fewer rotations"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    R["8"] --> L["3"]
    R --> Ri["10"]
    L --> LL["1"]
    L --> LR["6"]
    LR --> LRL["4"]
    LR --> LRR["7"]
    Ri --> RiR["14"]
    note["BST Rule: Left < Node < Right\nIn-order: 1,3,4,6,7,8,10,14"]
algorithms.keyInsight: BST validation bug: never check left.val < root.val < right.val locally — pass MIN and MAX bounds recursively. Delete with 2 children: find in-order successor (leftmost in right subtree), copy value, delete successor. Worst case O(n) on sorted input — always mention AVL/Red-Black as the fix.
algorithms.patternSignal: BST search insert delete, validate BST, kth smallest, in-order sorted, floor ceiling, BST property, sorted dynamic set
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Validate Binary Search Tree","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Pass min/max bounds recursively — local check is insufficient"},{"name":"Kth Smallest Element in BST","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"In-order gives sorted output — count nodes until kth"},{"name":"Convert Sorted Array to BST","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Mid of sorted array becomes root — recursively build balanced BST"},{"name":"Delete Node in a BST","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find in-order successor (leftmost in right subtree) for two-child case"},{"name":"Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Both < root → go left; both > root → go right; otherwise root IS LCA"}]

algorithms.name: Linked List: Runner Technique
algorithms.analogy: Two runners on a circular track — the fast one (2x speed) catches the slow one exactly when there's a loop. On a straight track, the fast one reaches the end when the slow one is halfway.
algorithms.story: Floyd's Cycle Detection: imagine a rabbit and a tortoise running on a linked list. The rabbit moves 2 nodes per step, the tortoise 1. If there's a cycle, the rabbit runs in circles and the tortoise catches up — they MUST meet inside the cycle. If no cycle, the rabbit reaches null first.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Detect a cycle in a linked list
• Find the middle node of a linked list
• Find the kth node from the end
• Detect where a cycle begins
• Rearrange linked list (first half + reversed second half)
• Check if linked list is a palindrome
algorithms.howItWorks:
Cycle Detection (Floyd):
1. slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null and fast.next != null:
3.   slow = slow.next
4.   fast = fast.next.next
5.   If slow == fast → cycle detected
6. If loop ends without meeting → no cycle

Find Middle:
1. Same slow/fast setup
2. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle

Find Cycle Start:
1. After slow==fast, reset slow to head
2. Move both one step at a time
3. Where they meet again = cycle start
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Always check fast != null && fast.next != null — fast.next.next would NPE if fast.next is null
• Cycle start proof: when slow and fast meet, reset slow to head and move both one step — they meet at cycle entry (mathematical proof using cycle length)
• Reverse linked list is the single most common linked list operation — memorize the 3-pointer technique
• Palindrome: find middle, reverse second half, compare, restore (always restore in an interview!)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"OS Memory Leak Detection","desc":"Operating system garbage collectors use cycle detection variants to find reference cycles in heap memory."},{"title":"Distributed Systems Loop Detection","desc":"Network routing protocols detect routing loops using variants of the runner technique on the path graph."},{"title":"Git Merge Base","desc":"Finding the common ancestor of two branches is similar to finding where two linked lists (commit histories) converge."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["NPE: checking fast != null but forgetting fast.next != null before accessing fast.next.next","Infinite loop: if you advance fast before checking slow==fast inside the loop, you may skip the meeting point","Not restoring the list after palindrome check — interviewers notice this","Finding middle of even-length list: slow stops at second-middle — decide if that's correct for your problem"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Kth from End","desc":"Advance fast k steps first, then move both together — when fast reaches end, slow is at kth from end"},{"name":"Merge Two Sorted Lists","desc":"Use a dummy head node and two pointers advancing through each list — no runner needed but same O(n) space pattern"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph LR
    A["head"] --> B["1"]
    B --> C["2"]
    C --> D["3"]
    D --> E["4"]
    E --> F["5"]
    F -.->|"cycle"| C
    S1["slow=1\nfast=1"] --> S2["slow=2\nfast=3"]
    S2 --> S3["slow=3\nfast=5"]
    S3 --> S4["slow=4\nfast=3 ← MEET!"]
algorithms.keyInsight: When fast reaches the end, slow has covered HALF the distance. Always check fast != null AND fast.next != null before accessing fast.next.next. For palindrome: find middle, reverse second half, compare, restore — always restore in interviews to prove you care about code correctness.
algorithms.patternSignal: detect cycle linked list, find middle, kth from end, palindrome linked list, cycle entry point, fast slow pointers, Floyd's algorithm
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Linked List Cycle","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Classic fast/slow — return true when they meet"},{"name":"Linked List Cycle II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Phase 1: detect meeting point inside cycle. Phase 2: reset slow to head, advance both at speed 1"},{"name":"Find the Duplicate Number","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Treat array as linked list where arr[i] = next node — duplicate is the cycle entry"},{"name":"Palindrome Linked List","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare, restore"},{"name":"Reorder List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, interleave the two halves"}]
